old_claim.note: Fine Arts (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because musical notation and early organum are a writing, mathematics, chant, and scriptorium tradition; the corrected fine_arts_medieval node is a later visual-art scope.

source_shape.source_locator: The Met anchors Romanesque art to the eleventh and twelfth centuries and describes visual-art media such as sculpture, frescoes, precious objects, and illuminated books.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source-backed fine_arts_medieval scope is a later visual-art complex, not a prerequisite for ninth-century music notation and polyphony.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes the direct edge because the music node is already grounded in writing, mathematics, and scriptoria, while Romanesque/Gothic visual art begins later than its first-known date.

ontology_before: Musical Notation & Polyphony depended on broad Fine Arts (Medieval), conflating visual fine arts with music notation and creating a chronology conflict after rescoping.
ontology_after: Musical Notation & Polyphony depends on writing, mathematics, and scriptoria, without a direct Romanesque/Gothic visual-art prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: musical_notation_polyphony no longer directly depends on fine_arts_medieval.
would_reject_if: fine_arts_medieval were broadened back to all medieval arts including music.
musical_notation_polyphony were rescoped to illustrated music manuscripts after 1000 rather than notation and early polyphony.
A source showed early organum or staff notation depended on Romanesque/Gothic visual art.
short_reason: Notation and early polyphony are not dependent on later Romanesque/Gothic visual art.
